critic_raw:
This experiment tests multi-modal prediction (genomic + tabular clinical + BioBERT embeddings) versus genomic-only baseline for immunotherapy response in melanoma, using n≈500 patients.

The most severe problem is sample size. With 500 patients, a 60/20/20 split yields approximately 100 test patients. The expected response rate for checkpoint inhibitor therapy in melanoma is approximately 30–40% (CR+PR). This means the test set has approximately 30–40 positive cases. A 95% bootstrap confidence interval on AUROC with 30–40 positives spans roughly ±0.08–0.12 AUROC units. The experiment proposes additional subgroup analyses (by stage, ECOG, TMB quartile, BRAF status) on the test set — a 4-way stratification on 100 patients yields subgroups of 25 patients on average, with approximately 8–10 positives each. These subgroup AUROCs are essentially uninterpretable. The experiment is statistically underpowered for its primary claim and severely underpowered for any subgroup analysis.

The baseline is 'identical in architecture but uses genomic features only, excluding clinical tabular and text branches.' This means the baseline has lower architectural capacity than the multi-modal model (fewer branches, fewer parameters). Any performance difference could be attributed to model capacity rather than information content. A fair baseline should either use the full architecture with only the genomic branch active (with zeros or masks for other branches) or a simpler model entirely. The current design conflates modality contribution with model capacity.

BioBERT embeddings are described as 'pretrained on biomedical text' and 'not retrained on this cohort.' For pathology reports and treatment summaries, domain-specific terminology (e.g., RECIST criteria, immunohistochemistry staining patterns) is well-represented in biomedical pretraining corpora. However, the specific linguistic patterns in single-institution clinical notes may diverge substantially from the pretraining distribution. Using fixed frozen BioBERT embeddings assumes the 768-dimensional representation captures the relevant semantics of these notes, which is an empirical question not validated anywhere in the design.

Stratified random splitting is used rather than temporal splitting on the grounds that 'temporal ordering reflects documentation sequence, not predictive information flow.' This argument fails for a multi-site dataset: if treatment protocols change over time (e.g., combination immunotherapy becoming standard partway through the study), a random split will mix pre- and post-protocol-change patients across folds, inflating apparent generalizability. Additionally, recent patients may be lost to follow-up for the 12-week RECIST evaluation, introducing non-random missingness that a random split will distribute equally across folds by accident rather than by design.
all_issues_raised: With ~100 test patients and ~30–40 expected responders, AUROC confidence intervals span ±0.08–0.12, and subgroup analyses on 25-patient strata with ~10 positives are statistically uninterpretable. The experiment is underpowered for its primary claim.

The baseline excludes clinical tabular and text branches, making it architecturally smaller than the multi-modal model. Performance differences could reflect model capacity rather than information content; no capacity-matched baseline (e.g., genomic-only branch with same total parameter budget) is included.

Frozen BioBERT embeddings are applied to single-institution clinical notes without validation that the pretrained representations capture institution-specific terminology or the specific note types (pathology reports, treatment summaries) relevant to immunotherapy response.

Stratified random splitting mixed with potential temporal protocol changes means pre- and post-protocol patients are distributed across train and test, inflating apparent generalizability compared to what would be observed in prospective deployment on future patients under new protocols.
all_issues_adjudicated: With ~100 test patients and ~30–40 expected responders, AUROC confidence intervals span ±0.08–0.12, and subgroup analyses on 25-patient strata with ~10 positives are statistically uninterpretable. The experiment is underpowered for its primary claim.

The baseline excludes clinical tabular and text branches, making it architecturally smaller than the multi-modal model. Performance differences could reflect model capacity rather than information content; no capacity-matched baseline (e.g., genomic-only branch with same total parameter budget) is included.

Frozen BioBERT embeddings are applied to single-institution clinical notes without validation that the pretrained representations capture institution-specific terminology or the specific note types (pathology reports, treatment summaries) relevant to immunotherapy response.

Stratified random splitting mixed with potential temporal protocol changes means pre- and post-protocol patients are distributed across train and test, inflating apparent generalizability compared to what would be observed in prospective deployment on future patients under new protocols.
